A.name: The CTA Leak
A.desc: Plain-English booking-process angle. Leads with the $5K-$15K/week leak. Three issues, tease the third. Close with calendar or sample audit.

beats.title: Booking Process Leak
beats.line: Hi, my name is {caller} with Conversion Exotics — I review exotic and luxury car rental sites for a living. I pulled up your site, and your booking process is costing you roughly $5,000 to $15,000 a week in booked rentals. Do you know who I can talk to about this?
responses: What do you mean costing me?
beats.followup: Got it — quick context on what I'm seeing, then I'll show you exactly where the money's going.
beats.note: Calling the business, not a person. No name tokens. No apology hedges.

beats.title: 3-Second Decision
beats.line: Quick context — when a luxury renter taps your ad from their phone, they decide in about 3 seconds whether you're worth waiting on. Every one-second delay cuts conversions roughly 32 percent. Most exotic rental sites load in 5 to 8 seconds on mobile. When was the last time someone on your team pulled your site up on a phone and booked a rental end-to-end?
beats.followup: Yeah — that's what most owners say. The site loading is just the first leak. The bigger ones come after.
beats.note: Plain English. Mobile speed as door-opener, not the main point.

beats.title: The Three Leaks
beats.line: Here's what's actually happening. A renter taps your ad, counts to three, and opens the next one — they never even see your fleet. The ones who wait it out land on a page that doesn't show a price, doesn't show the car they came for, and asks them to fill out a form before they've decided anything. By the time they figure out how to book, they've already opened two competitors in other tabs. That's the $5,000 to $15,000 a week. Cleaning up the top of the page and making the booking obvious — those are week-one quick wins. But there's a third issue on your booking process that costs more than both combined. I'd rather show you that one with your page open.
responses: How much would this cost to fix?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly what the audit answers. Pricing comes after.
beats.note: Tease the third issue. Do NOT reveal it on the call. The reveal is the audit.

beats.title: 100+ Sites, Same 3 Leaks
beats.line: I've done this on over 100 exotic and luxury car rental sites — same 10-point checklist, same booking process, same three leaks every time. The shops that fix the top two see bookings climb in the first week. The ones that fix all three usually double their weekend bookings inside 30 days.
responses: How long does the fix take?
beats.followup: Fair — and that's exactly why I lead with the audit, not the pitch.
beats.note: Credibility frame. 100+ sites. Same checklist. Pattern recognition.

beats.title: Free $3,000 Audit
beats.line: Here's what I want to do. I run a $3,000 conversion audit — and I'd like to do yours free. Thirty minutes. Your live site on screen. Every leak named in plain English no agency would dare use, with dollar-anchored estimates per leak. You walk away with a sequenced one-week plan — every fix shipped at once, ranked by booking impact. Yours to keep whether you hire me to ship it or not. Not vibes — math.
beats.followup: No catch — I'm running a handful of these personally this quarter for case studies. That's the only ask.
beats.note: Verbatim site language. $3K free. One-week plan. All fixes shipped at once.

beats.title: Calendar or Sample
beats.line: Two ways we can do this. I've got Thursday at 10 or Friday at 2 open — pick one and I'll send the calendar invite. Or, if you'd rather see what this looks like first, give me your email and I'll send over a sample audit I did for another exotic rental shop — full plan, dollar numbers, the whole thing. Which one would you prefer?
beats.followup: Done — calendar invite or sample audit hits your inbox in 60 seconds.
beats.note: Dual-path close. Booked call OR sample audit. Either is a win.

B.name: Curiosity Hook + Mobile Leak
B.desc: Soft permission. Curiosity-as-problem opener. Mobile-vs-desktop fleet gap.

beats.title: Curiosity Permission
beats.line: Hi there — {caller} with Conversion Exotics. I was hoping you could help me for a sec. I'm not sure you're even the right person, but are you the one who'd notice if your mobile site was quietly bleeding Ferrari and Lambo bookings before people ever hit the form?
beats.followup: It is — but a short one, and probably useful either way. Got 30 seconds?
beats.note: "Quietly bleeding" is the hook. Say it slow. Let it land.

beats.title: Mobile Reality Check
beats.line: Here's the part most owners don't see. 70+ percent of exotic rental searches happen on a phone, and most luxury sites still feel built for desktop. Out of curiosity, when you pull up your own site on mobile, does it actually feel like the fleet you've built?
beats.followup: Yeah — and that gap between the cars in your garage and the cars on the screen is usually where the money quietly walks out.
beats.note: Don't argue if they say "it's decent". Just plant the seed and move on.

beats.title: Window Shoppers vs Real Ones
beats.line: Because the renter who bounces isn't gone — they're on the exotic shop across town or the next ad inside two minutes. That's a $1,500–$3,500 day, sometimes a $10,000 weekend, going to someone with a worse fleet and a faster site. And the ones left? Window shoppers, not real ones. The buyers who'd actually book today bounced on page one. Would you be opposed to seeing where yours is leaking?
beats.followup: Totally fair — that's exactly what the audit answers.
beats.note: "Window shoppers vs real ones" is verbatim operator language. They will recognize it.

beats.title: Showroom Rebuild
beats.line: What I do is rebuild it as a digital showroom — showroom-style fleet display, pricing and availability you don't have to dig for, and mobile booking under 60 seconds. So the Lambo on the screen sells as hard as the one in your garage.
beats.followup: Pricing's the easy part later. The audit's the only thing that matters right now.
beats.note: If "we've tried agencies before" → "Yeah, most agencies do traffic. I do conversion. Different job."

beats.title: Free $3,000 Audit
beats.line: What I do first — whether we end up working together or not — is a free conversion audit, normally $3,000, yours to keep. Would it be unreasonable to spend 30 minutes walking you through the 3–5 biggest spots losing you bookings, what a conversion-built exotic site would change, and the extra bookings your current traffic could be producing?
responses: What do you need from me?
beats.followup: Almost nothing on your end — best email, and I do the rest live on the call.
beats.note: Audit is the door, not the close.

beats.title: Lock the Time
beats.line: Best email so I can send the calendar link — and would it be unreasonable to block 30 minutes tomorrow or the day after to walk through what I find?
beats.followup: Done — link's in your inbox in 60 seconds. Pick the first slot that works.
beats.note: Send calendar link immediately. Log call. Auto-reset.

C.name: Paid-Search Leak
C.desc: CPC anchor → window-shopper frame → DM-trap agitation → audit offer. Uses live keyword cost data.

beats.title: Spend Frame Disarm
beats.line: Hey — I'll be quick. I'm guessing you guys are running Google Ads for the Lambo and Ferrari side out of {market}, right?
beats.followup: Totally fair — I'll be quick. Got 30 seconds?
beats.note: If "we don't run ads" → "Got it — then this is even more interesting. Quick reason for the call…"

beats.title: CPC Anchor
beats.line: Cool — {caller} with Conversion Exotics. I pulled the keyword data before I called. {primary_kw} is running about ${cpc_low}–${cpc_high} per click right now. "Exotic car rental near me" tops $14.49 a click. Quick question — when's the last time you actually clicked your own ad on a phone and tried to book?
beats.followup: Yeah — that's the part most owners miss. The ad's not the problem.
beats.note: Real CPCs. Pulled from Google Ads Keyword Planner. Say them with confidence.

beats.title: The DM Trap + Window Shoppers
beats.line: Here's what's happening — you're paying $5–$10 a click to land people on a page where the only way to actually book is to DM you on Instagram or call the number. So the real buyers, the ones ready to drop $3,500 today, they bounce — they go to the next ad or the shop across town that lets them book in 60 seconds. What's left in your DMs? Window shoppers, tire kickers, midnight messages from kids who can't afford the deposit. Would you be opposed to seeing exactly where that ad spend is leaking?
beats.followup: Got it — and the leak is almost never where the analytics says it is.
beats.note: This is the killer beat. Slow down on "window shoppers". Verbatim VoC.

beats.title: Convert the Click
beats.line: What I do at Conversion Exotics is turn the page your ads land on into a digital showroom — live pricing, fleet that sells itself, deposit clarity up front, and a sub-60-second mobile booking. So the click you're already paying for actually closes instead of bouncing into a DM you'll answer at midnight.
responses: We're not changing the site right now
beats.followup: Totally fair — pricing's the easy part later. Audit first.
beats.note: Don't pitch the build. Pitch the audit.

beats.title: Free $3,000 Audit
beats.line: Whether we work together or not — I'll run a free conversion audit. Normally $3,000, yours to keep. Would it be unreasonable to spend 30 minutes walking you through exactly where the ad spend is leaking, what each click is actually worth, and the 3–5 fixes that close more renters from the traffic you're already paying for?
beats.followup: No catch — running a handful personally this quarter for case studies. That's the trade.
beats.note: Free audit is the door, not the close.

beats.title: Lock the Time
beats.line: Shoot me your best email so I can send the calendar link — and let's block 30 minutes tomorrow or the day after. I'll have the audit pulled up live, screen-share, the whole thing. Cool?
responses: I'll see if I can move some things
beats.followup: Done. Calendar link hits your inbox in 60 seconds. Pick whichever slot works.
beats.note: Open calendar embed for them right then if they're still on the line.
